C. (2004). ''Breeding birds in central Arabia 1978-2003''. Sandgrouse, 26(1), 35-47.</ref> Elsewhere in the [[Middle East]], their range includes eastern [[Iraq]] and west, south, and northeastern [[Iran]], extending somewhat into [[Turkmenistan]] in the [[Kopet Dag]] range.<ref name=iucn/><ref>Desfayes, M., & Praz, J. C. (1978). ''Notes on habitat and distribution of montane birds in southern Iran''. Bonner Zoologische Beiträge, 29(1), 18–37.</ref><ref>Bukreyev, S.A. (1998). [Materials on breeding of the Bonelli's Eagle (Hieraaetus fasciatus) in Kopetdagh, Turkmenistan]. Ornitologiya, 28: 154–158.</ref>

Further east into Asia, their distribution includes eastern [[Afghanistan]] and [[Pakistan]] through most of the [[Indian subcontinent]], where generally it is uncommon but more locally common near [[Nepal]]. On the other hand, they are absent in eastern India and only occur as a vagrant to [[Sri Lanka]] and [[Bangladesh]].<ref name=iucn/> In [[India]], they are most regularly found in areas such as the [[Chambal River|Chambal ravines]], [[Ranthambore National Park]], the Chir zone of the lower [[Kumaon division|Kumaun Himalayas]], and in winter in the [[Keoladeo National Park]] of [[Bharatpur, Rajasthan]].<ref name= Naoroji/> From central [[Myanmar]], they range across into northwestern [[Thailand]] and northern [[Laos]] (though possibly only as a visitor rather than breeding in the latter two). In southern [[China]], their resident range includes [[Yunnan]], [[Guangxi]], and [[Guangdong]] north to the [[Yangtze River]], as well as rarely into [[Hong Kong]].<ref name=iucn/><ref>Round, P. D. (1983). ''Some recent bird records from northern Thailand''. Nat. Hist. Bull. Siam Soc, 31(2), 123–138.</ref><ref>Scharringa, J. (1994). ''A record of the Bonelli's Eagle Hieraaetus fasciatus in Thailand''. Natural History Bulletin of the Siam Society, 42: 291.</ref><ref>MacKinnon, J. R., MacKinnon, J., Phillipps, K., & He, F. Q. (2000). ''A field guide to the birds of China''. Oxford University Press.</ref><ref>Carey, G. (2001). ''The Avifauna of Hong Kong''. Hong Kong Bird Watching Society.</ref> Their isolated [[Indonesia]]n population range is in the [[Lesser Sunda Islands]], including at least [[Sumbawa]], [[Timor]], [[Wetar]], [[Maluku (province)|Luang]], and [[Flores]], however records show they've turned up on as many as 20 islands in the Lesser Sundas.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Trainor/><ref>Trainor, C. R. (2002). ''The birds of Adonara, Lesser Sundas, Indonesia''. Forktail, 93–100.</ref>

Historically speaking, research published in ''[[British Ecological Society#Publications|People and Nature]]'' in 2024 by scientists from the [[University of Granada]] and [[Miguel Hernández University of Elche]] (UMH) indicates that Bonelli's eagle is a relative newcomer to Europe and the [[Mediterranean Basin]] and that they spread there with the help of humans around 50,000 years ago.<ref name=phys>{{cite web |url=https://phys.org/news/2024-05-homo-sapiens-bonelli-eagle-mediterranean.html |title=Homo sapiens facilitated establishment of Bonelli's eagle in the Mediterranean 50,000 years ago, study finds |last=Gallar |first=A. |publisher=[[Miguel Hernández University of Elche]] |website=phys.org |date=2024-05-21 |access-date=2024-05-29}}</ref><ref name=people2024>{{cite journal |title=Wildlife following people: A multidisciplinary assessment of the ancient colonization of the Mediterranean Basin by a long-lived raptor |journal=People and Nature |last1=Moleón |first1=M. |last2=Graciá |first2=E. |last3=García |first3=N. |last4=Gil-Sánchez |first4=J.M. |last5=Godinho |first5=R. |last6=Beja |first6=P. |last7=Palma |first7=L. |last8=Real |first8=J. |last9=Hernández-Matías |first9=A. |last10=Muñoz |first10=A.R. |last11=Arrondo |first11=E. |last12=Sánchez-Zapata |first12=J.A. |volume= 6|issue= 3|pages= 1303–1319|doi=10.1002/pan3.10642 |date=2024|doi-access=free |bibcode=2024PeoNa...6.1303M |hdl=10261/388196 |hdl-access=free}}</ref> Genetic analyses indicate that the Mediterranean Bonelli's eagle population likely comprised a few individuals around the last glacial maximum, which later thrived as the temperature in the Mediterranean  Basin rose, and the human population grew and became sedentary.<ref name=phys/>

Mechanistically, the three bilateral interactions among Bonelli's eagles, [[golden eagles]], and the human population are jointly what allowed Bonelli's eagles to move from the [[Middle East]] into the Mediterranean Basin at this time – and also what prevented them from moving there sooner.<ref name=phys/><ref name=people2024/> Golden eagles are less tolerant of humans than Bonelli's eagles are.<ref name=phys/> Additionally, between the two eagle species, the golden eagle is the dominant species, while Bonelli's eagle is the subordinate one.<ref name=phys/> With these interactions in mind, the authors of the 2024 ''People and Nature'' study hypothesize and test with data-driven mathematical models that with the arrival of the first anatomically modern humans in Europe, some of the golden eagle territories closest to human settlements were abandoned, and these 'vacant' territories began to be occupied by Bonelli's eagles from the Middle East.<ref name=phys/><ref name=people2024/> That is, Bonelli's eagles could not have established themselves in the Mediterranean before the arrival of the first Homo sapiens because the competitive pressure exerted by golden eagles and other species would have been too overwhelming, but the arrival of humans displaced the golden eagles and provided an empty niche in the localities' ecosystems that Bonelli's eagles were then able to neatly fill.<ref name=phys/><ref name=people2024/>

Bonelli's eagles are mostly residential throughout their range but juvenile can disperse up to over several hundred kilometres. Sometimes, they are recorded at migration sites and at spots where not known to breed in winter.<ref>Franco, A. (1980). ''Observacion de Hieraaetus fasciatus en una corriente migratoria otonal de rapaces en Ceuta''. Donana, Acta Vertebrat, 7: 263.</ref><ref>Billet, J. (1994). ''Aigle de Bonelli''. Survie et resistance en region Paca. 25: 18.</ref> Wanderings include around {{cvt|700|km}} north of their regular range in France near the coast of [[English Channel]], far from their normal haunts in [[Regensburg]], [[Germany]] and, probably both from the Italian island populations, to northwestern [[Italy]] and [[Slovenia]].<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref>Klose, A. (1979). ''Habichtsadler Hieraaetus fasciatus bei Regensburg''. Anzeiger der Ornithologischen Gesellschaft in Bayern, 17: 177–178.</ref><ref>Alessandria, G., & Boano, G. (2011). ''Le comparse dell'aquila di Bonelli Aquila fasciata in Italia nord-occidentale: eventi eccezionali o normale erratismo''. Avocetta, 35, 3–12.</ref><ref>Scott, R. E. (1997). ''Opazovanje kraguljega orla Hieraaetus fasciatus junija 1997 pri Predjamskem gradu''. Acrocephalus, 18: 98–99.</ref> From their [[Iberian Peninsula]] range presumably, vagrants have been reported in the [[Canary Islands]].<ref>Clarke, T. (2006). ''Field guide to the birds of the Atlantic islands''. Christopher Helm.</ref> Beyond Sri Lanka, other areas the species has been known to vagrate (or perhaps rarely winter) in Asia have included [[Kazakhstan]], the [[Korean Peninsula]], [[Malaysia]] and [[Cochinchina]] in [[Vietnam]], as well as a record in winter 1996 on the isle of [[Yamdena]], the latter presumably from the Lesser Sunda population.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Trainor/><ref>Wassink, A., & Oreel, G. J. (2007). ''The birds of Kazakhstan''. A. Wassink.</ref><ref>Lee, W. S., Ku, T. H., & Park, J. Y. (2000). ''A field guide to the birds of Korea''. LG Evergreen Foundation.</ref><ref>Tordoff, A. W., & Eames, J. C. (2001). ''New additions to the list of birds of Vietnam''. Oriental Bird Club Bulletin, 33, 37–38.</ref><ref>Jeyarajasingam, A., & Pearson, A. (1999). ''A field guide to the birds of West Malaysia and Singapore''. Oxford University Press.</ref>

===Habitat===
Bonelli's eagle usually lives at an elevation of {{cvt|1500|m}} or lower in Europe, in the [[Himalayas]] at {{cvt|1200|-|2000|m}} and up to {{cvt|3750|m}} in [[Bhutan]], at {{cvt|2000|m}} in the [[Atlas Mountains]].<ref name=Ferguson-Lees/><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Thiollay |first=J.-M. |date=1980 |title=L'évolution des peuplements d'oiseaux le long d'un gradient altitudinal dans l'Himalaya central |url=https://www.persee.fr/doc/revec_0249-7395_1980_num_34_2_4062 |journal=Revue d'Écologie (La Terre et la Vie) |language=fr |volume=34 |issue=2 |pages=199–269 |doi=10.3406/revec.1980.4062 |doi-access=free}}</ref>
It tends to dwell in similar habitat types across its range and lives in lands hugging large bodies of water, largely the [[Mediterranean Sea]] and northern [[Indian Ocean]]. To a lesser extent, it lives near the coast of the [[Atlantic]] and the [[Pacific Ocean]]s and near the [[Caspian Sea]]. Despite often being near seas and oceans it mostly occurs in fairly [[Aridity|arid]] habitats and in quite sunny areas. It prefers [[Rock (geology)|rocky]] areas including lower mountains, [[foothills]] with plentiful [[cliff]]s and steep sided [[canyon]]s; it is very skilled at hunting in craggy, irregular rocky terrain.<ref name=Ferguson-Lees/><ref name=Naoroji/><ref name=Baccetti>Baccetti, N. & Spagnesi, M. (1987). ''Rapaci Mediterranei III: Atti del Quarto Colloquio Internazionale sui Rapaci Mediterranei''. Supplemento alle Ricerche di Biologia della Selvaggina, 12: 1–316.</ref> Usually, extensive [[garrigue]]-type habitat such as low [[bushes]] or more substantial vegetation such as scattered trees are a common feature of residential ranges but also at times even denser [[woodland]]s. Such scrubby areas are key since they hold prey concentration in Mediterranean habitats.<ref name=Carrascal>{{Cite journal |last1=Carrascal |first1=L.M. |last2=Seoane |first2=J. |date=2009 |title=Factors affecting large-scale distribution of the Bonelli's eagle ''Aquila fasciata'' in Spain |journal=Ecological Research |volume=24 |issue=3 |pages=565–573 |doi=10.1007/s11284-008-0527-8 |bibcode=2009EcoR...24..565C |s2cid=15711835}}</ref><ref name=Real>{{Cite journal |last1=Real |first1=J. |last2=Bosch |first2=R. |last3=Tintó |first3=A. |last4=Hernández-Matías |first4=A. |date=2016 |title=Identifying key habitats for the conservation of Bonelli's Eagle ''Aquila fasciata'' using radiotracking |journal=Ibis |volume=158 |issue=3 |pages=556–568 |doi=10.1111/ibi.12372}}</ref> However, excessive ground cover may limit hunting success, so it avoids dense scrub.<ref name=Carrascal/><ref name="Martinez-Miranzo">{{Cite journal |last1=Martínez-Miranzo |first1=B. |last2=Banda |first2=E.I. |last3=Aguirre |first3=J.I. |date=2016 |title=Multiscale analysis of habitat selection by Bonelli's eagle (''Aquila fasciata'') in NE Spain |journal=European Journal of Wildlife Research |volume=62 |issue=6 |pages=673–679 |doi=10.1007/s10344-016-1041-x |bibcode=2016EJWR...62..673M |s2cid=254182824}}</ref> In the Mediterranean region, it visits [[Pinus|pine]] or [[sclerophyll]] forests, but avoids deep forests.<ref name= Real/><ref name="Vittorio">{{Cite journal |last1=Di Vittorio |first1=M. |last2=S. |first2=M. |last3=López-López |first3=P. |date=2012 |title=Habitat preferences of Bonelli's Eagles ''Aquila fasciata'' in Sicily |journal=Bird Study |volume=59 |issue=2 |pages=207–217 |doi=10.1080/00063657.2012.656577 |bibcode=2012BirdS..59..207D |hdl=10447/77043 |s2cid=59319642 |hdl-access=free}}</ref> In Spain and [[Sicily]], it shows a preference for agricultural [[arable land]] and other human-modified habitats, probably because it preys on [[feral pigeon]]s.<ref name=Martinez-Miranzo/><ref name=Vittorio/> However, it avoids [[urban areas]].<ref name=Real/> It may additionally range into timbered [[plain]]s or even virtually barren slopes or [[semi-desert]], especially in [[Israel]] and [[India]] where moister [[valley]]s intersect with [[desert]]s.<ref name=Ferguson-Lees/><ref name=Real/> Juveniles may take up temporary residence over dry cultivation, small [[wetland]] areas, [[coast]]lines or surprisingly deep woodlands.<ref name=Ferguson-Lees/><ref name=Naoroji/>

In winter, Bonelli's eagle occurs at lower elevations and more open habitats in semi-deserts and plains, where it can appear surprisingly at home; it often prefers wetter habitats such as large [[river]] mouths, [[marsh]]lands and [[lake]]s, especially where these fall in existing home range, as prey is more likely to be concentrated in such areas.<ref name=Carrascal/> In some of the Indian subcontinent, Southeast Asia and the Lesser Sundas, Bonelli's eagle inhabits [[tropical rainforest]] that is much wetter and more humid than their typical habitats, and in such areas it is attracted to more sparse and rocky areas such as slopes and cliffs as well as alternately open mosaics and [[Glade (geography)|glades]].<ref name=Ferguson-Lees/><ref name=Naoroji/>

==Behaviour and ecology ==
[[File:Bonelli's Eagle Pair from Raigad.jpg|thumb|Pair of Bonelli's eagle at Raigad]]
This species is very aerial, often given to soaring and circling over its home range. Like most raptorial birds, it mainly lives solitarily or in a breeding pair.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Naoroji/> Bonelli's eagle is a powerful predator, and has been described as rather "bold and rapacious".<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Brown2/> Its primary hunting methods recall those of a powerful ''[[Accipiter]]'' such as a [[Northern goshawk|goshawk]]. Most commonly, this eagle still-hunts, often either utilizing a concealed tree perch or a lofty spot in irregular rocky terrain to watch for prey activity. Upon spotting its quarry, it often dashes out rapidly to take birds as they take off or a mammal as it runs for cover, at times making lengthy tail-chase that may continue between trees or into tree stands or bushes. Not infrequently as a latter part of a tail chase, these eagles (again reminiscent of a goshawk) will occasionally walk on the ground to obtain their prey.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Whistler/><ref name= Marmasse>Marmasse, A. & Vilatte, M. (1995). ''[Prey capture and aerial acrobatics of Bonelli's Eagle Hieraaetus fasicatus]''. Faune de Provence, 16: 123.</ref> Bonelli's eagles also hunt in a quartering flying style relatively close to the ground (in a fashion reminiscent of a [[Harrier (bird)|harrier]]) or patrols hillsides for prey activity.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Marmasse/> 

Bonelli's eagles will also occasionally stoop from a soaring height onto prey. Mostly, this predator takes birds from on or near the ground but some snatched them from bushes and, seldom, water. It has been known to have sufficient agility to catch some birds from active flight.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Whistler/><ref name= Marmasse/> In one case, a Bonelli's eagle was observed to fly below a [[Western jackdaw|jackdaw]] and swoop upwards to grab it from below.<ref name= Marmasse/> [[Pack hunter|Tandem hunting]] by a lifelong pair is quite common, almost exceptionally so in this species. One eagle tends to fly directly above the other, with several cases of one eagle scattering a bird flock for the other eagle to quickly single out, in a similar style to tandem-hunting [[laggar falcon]]s (''Falco jugger''). However, per Spanish studies, apparently tandem hunting neither improved hunting success nor were the eagles able to capture larger prey (in fact the estimated prey size by pairs was slightly lower than that taken by each mate hunting by itself) while hunting in tandem. It was hypothesized that tandem hunting is more important to the socio-sexual relations of the pair rather than capture of a significant amount of prey.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Watve>Watve, M.G., Sant, N.R. & Joshi, V. (1995). ''Why Bonelli's Eagles hunt in pair: an assessment of individual and paired hunting successes''. Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society, 91: 355–359.</ref><ref name=Martinez>{{Cite journal |last1=Martínez |first1=J. |last2=Zuberogoitia |first2=I. |last3=Gómez |first3=G. |last4=Escarabajal |first4=J. |last5=Cerezo |first5=E. |last6=Jiménez-Franco |first6=M. |last7=Calvo |first7=J. F. |date=2014 |title=Attack success in Bonelli's Eagle ''Aquila fasciata'' |journal=Ornis Fennica |volume=91 |issue=2 |doi=10.51812/of.133845 |doi-access=free}}</ref> Compared to most other [[Aquilinae|booted eagles]], Bonelli's eagle takes a great majority of its prey alive and seldom comes to carrion or [[Kleptoparasitism|pirates]] foods from other raptors.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Brown2/> However, it will readily come to previously injured prey, especially [[water bird]]s shot by [[Hunting|duck hunters]], and will readily take young animals across most prey classes.<ref name= Naoroji/> Also, in [[Keoladeo National Park]], [[India]], Bonelli's eagles were observed to habitually follow [[Harrier (bird)|harrier]]s, [[spotted eagle]]s and other ''[[Aquila (genus)|Aquila]]'' eagles in order to capture [[water bird]]s incidentally flushed during their flybys.<ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Prakash>Prakash, V. (1988). ''The general ecology of raptors in Keoladeo National Park, Bharatpur'' (Doctoral dissertation, Ph. D. thesis. Bombay University, Mumbai, India).</ref>
[[File:Bonelli's Eagle with prey - Montsonis - Spain MG 4679 (24590808223).jpg|thumb|A Bonelli's eagle that has caught a [[rock dove]], one of the favoured prey species.]]
Overall, Bonelli's eagles take a fairly wide variety of prey. Across its wide range, their prey spectrum has been known to include perhaps up to nearly 200 prey species. Dietary studies have primarily been conducted in [[western Europe]], though some study has gone into their food habits elsewhere (being well known in [[Cyprus]] and, less so, [[India]]).<ref name= Moleon/><ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Iezekiel>Iezekiel, S., Bakaloudis, D. E., & Vlachos, C. G. (2004). ''The diet of the Bonelli's eagle Hieraaetus fasciatus in Cyprus''. In: Raptors worldwide: proceedings of the VI world conference on birds of prey and owls. Berlin: World Working Group on Birds of Prey/MME (pp. 581–87).</ref> Brown & Amadon (1986) considered Bonelli's eagles prey size range as nearly as extensive as the most massive [[Aquilinae|booted eagles]], such as the golden eagle and the [[martial eagle]] (''Polemaetus bellicosus'') (but mainly may have been describing the [[African hawk-eagle]] that was lumped at the time).<ref name= Brown/> Bonelli's eagles mainly hunts [[bird]]s and [[mammal]]s, taking [[reptile]]s and other prey types on a more local and sporadic basis. In [[western Europe]], it is considered something of a specialist predator on [[European rabbit|rabbits]] and [[Red-legged partridge|partridges]], though other birds such as [[pigeon]]s, [[Yellow-legged gull|gulls]] and [[corvid]]s sometimes are taken as much or more so depending on local prey population trends.<ref name= Moleon/> 

Pellet analysis is considered the most reliable way to get a complete picture of Bonelli's eagle dietary habits.<ref name=Martinez/> Despite its predaceous power, typically the average size of prey taken are within average range for a raptorial bird and it may take smaller prey on average than its mildly smaller cousin, the [[African hawk-eagle]].<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Steyn/> In [[Sierra Morena]], [[Spain]], the mean size of prey taken was estimated at {{cvt|630|g}}, while in [[Greece]] the mean prey size was estimated at {{cvt|877|g}}.<ref name="Jordano">{{Cite journal |last=Jordano |first=Pedro |date=1981-12-01 |title=Relaciones interespecificas y coexistencia entre el Águila Real (Aquila chrysaetos) y el Águila Perdicera (''Hieraaetus fasciatus'') en sierra Morena central |journal=Ardeola |volume=28 |pages=67–88|s2cid=127627229 }}</ref><ref name= Alivizatos>Alivizatos, H. & Bourdakis, S. ''Diet and breeding success of the Bonelli's Eagle (Hieraaetus fasciatus) in Greece: preliminary data''. International Hawkwatcher, 5: 3–6.</ref> A subsequent study in Spain, however, posited the mean prey size as lower than in the past, stating that prey taken by males averaged an estimated {{cvt|416|g}} and by females at {{cvt|459|g}}, probably due to increased importance of [[pigeon]]s and reduced numbers of [[rabbit]]s.<ref name= Martinez/> Thus, on average, prey sizes average about 20–45% of Bonelli's eagles own weight.<ref name= Martinez/><ref name= Jordano/><ref name= Alivizatos/><ref name="Marti">{{Cite book |last1=Marti |first1=Carl D. |url=http://link.springer.com/10.1007/978-1-4615-9582-3_2 |title=Trophic Structure of Raptor Communities: A Three-Continent Comparison and Synthesis |last2=Korpimäki |first2=Erkki |last3=Jaksić |first3=Fabian M. |date=1993 |publisher=Springer US |isbn=978-1-4615-9584-7 |editor-last=Power |editor-first=D. M. |location=Boston, MA |pages=47–137 |doi=10.1007/978-1-4615-9582-3_2}}</ref> Furthermore, the latter Spanish study found hunting success of Bonelli's eagles to average around 28.5%, a slightly higher hunting success rate than [[golden eagle]]s (20%) or [[lesser spotted eagle]]s (''Clanga pomarina'') (24%) but slightly lower than [[greater spotted eagle]]s (''Clanga clanga'') (34%).<ref name= Martinez/>

===Rabbits and other lagomorphs===
[[File:Katoenstaartkonijn 1.jpg|thumb|left|[[European rabbit]]s are strongly favoured prey for Bonelli's eagles in [[western Europe]].]]
More than any other, the [[European rabbit]] (''Oryctolagus cuniculus'') is considered the most important prey species for most European Bonelli's eagles.<ref name= Moleon/> In the largest European studies, the rabbit is usually the leading prey species: such as in [[Catalonia]], [[Spain]] where rabbits comprised 22.54% of 2254 prey items (and 33.3%  of the prey biomass) and in [[Provence]], [[France]] where rabbits made up 16.4% of 2742 prey items.<ref name= Mayor>Mayor, J.R. (2014). ''Study of the Feeding Ecology of Bonelli's Eagle: Effects of Diet on Body Condition, Vital Rates and Demography''. Universitat de Barcelona (Doctoral dissertation)</ref><ref name="Morvan">Morvan, R. (2010). ''Aigle de Bonelli (Hieraaetus fasciatus) : présentation de l'espèce et des causes de son déclin''. Rev. sci. Bourgogne-Nature, 11: 228–235.</ref><ref name=Ontiveros>{{Cite journal |last1=Ontiveros |first1=D. |last2=Pleguezuelos |first2=J. M. |last3=Caro |first3=J. |date=2005 |title=Prey density, prey detectability and food habits: the case of Bonelli's eagle and the conservation measures |journal=Biological Conservation |volume=123 |issue=1 |pages=19–25 |doi=10.1016/j.biocon.2004.10.004|bibcode=2005BCons.123...19O }}</ref> In the third largest western European study, rabbits were secondary in number to pigeons (at 18.4% of 1641 prey items) but were still the largest contributors of biomass, at 33.2%.<ref name="Palma">{{Cite journal |last1=Palma |first1=L. |last2=Beja |first2=P. |last3=Pais |first3=M. |last4=Cancela Da Fonseca |first4=L. |date=2006 |title=Why do raptors take domestic prey? The case of Bonelli's eagles and pigeons |journal=Journal of Applied Ecology |volume=43 |issue=6 |pages=1075–1086 |doi=10.1111/j.1365-2664.2006.01213.x |doi-access=free|bibcode=2006JApEc..43.1075P |hdl=10400.1/11235 |hdl-access=free}}</ref> Even where non-native, such as the [[Aegean islands]] of [[Greece]], the European rabbit dominated the foods of this eagle, comprising 40.8% by number and 46.6% by biomass of the foods.<ref name= Alivizatos/> In Spain, it was found that about three-quarters of studied floating juvenile Bonelli's eagles were hunting rabbits almost exclusively, apparently as they were easier to capture despite their larger size than bird prey.<ref name="Caro">{{Cite journal |last1=Caro |first1=J. |last2=Ontiveros |first2=D. |last3=Pleguezuelos |first3=J. M. |date=August 2011 |title=The feeding ecology of Bonelli's eagle (Aquila fasciata) floaters in southern Spain: implications for conservation |journal=European Journal of Wildlife Research |volume=57 |issue=4 |pages=729–736 |doi=10.1007/s10344-010-0480-z |bibcode=2011EJWR...57..729C |s2cid=12129733 |url=https://hal.science/hal-00656344 }}</ref> 

Research determines that Bonelli's eagle are often attracted to scrub areas during hunting forays to catch sight of rabbits foraying out of vegetative cover. Since young juvenile and yearling rabbits are forced out to more open feeding spots by dominant adult rabbits, they are disproportionately often selected by Bonelli's eagles and other avian predators. Rabbits become more commonly caught during the summer when the young rabbits tend to disperse.<ref name= Martinez/><ref name= Moleon/><ref name="Palomares">{{Cite journal |last1=Palomares |first1=F. |last2=Delibes |first2=M. |date=1997 |title=Predation upon European Rabbits and Their Use of Open and Closed Patches in Mediterranean Habitats |journal=Oikos |volume=80 |issue=2 |page=407 |doi=10.2307/3546609|jstor=3546609 |bibcode=1997Oikos..80..407P }}</ref> On the contrary, 86.2% of the rabbits taken in southwest Portugal were reportedly adults.<ref name= Palma/> Most rabbits caught by Bonelli's eagle were estimated to weigh between {{cvt|500|and|1500|g}} (from the size of a kit to a smallish adult) per Spanish studies, with an estimated average weight in Spain of {{cvt|857|g}}.<ref name= Moleon/><ref name= Mayor/> A study in southeastern Spain estimated that the region's Bonelli's eagles claim about 337 rabbits during the breeding season and 237 rabbits during non-breeding during the course of a year, so despite their heavy predation barely make a dent on the overall population of rabbits (effecting less than 2.5% of the population at peak).<ref name= Moleon3/> The native western European population of wild rabbit has been heavily depleted by [[myxomatosis]] and [[rabbit haemorrhagic disease]], having been reduced by an estimated 50–70%. While the overall numbers seemingly taken by them reduced by as much as a third between 1968 and 2009, on evidence Bonelli's eagle still sought them out and hunted rabbits preferentially even during the non-breeding season when their numbers dip to their lowest.<ref name="Moleon3">{{Cite journal |last1=Moleón |first1=M. |last2=Gil-Sánchez |first2=J. M. |last3=Real |first3=Joan |last4=Antonio |first4=J. |last5=Bautista |first5=Jesús |last6=Sánchez-Clemot |first6=José Francisco |date=2007 |title=Non-breeding feeding ecology of territorial Bonelli's eagles ''Hieraaetus fasciatus'' in the Iberian Peninsula |url=https://www.ardeola.org/uploads/articles/docs/1311.pdf |journal=Ardeola |volume=54 |issue=1 |pages=135–143}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Moleón |first1=Marcos |last2=Sánchez-Zapata |first2=José Antonio |last3=Real |first3=Joan |last4=García-Charton |first4=J. A. |last5=Gil-Sánchez |first5=J. M. |last6=Palma |first6=L. |last7=Bautista |first7=J. |last8=Bayle |first8=P. |date=2009 |title=Large-scale spatio-temporal shifts in the diet of a predator mediated by an emerging infectious disease of its main prey |journal=Journal of Biogeography |volume=36 |issue=8 |pages=1502–1515 |doi=10.1111/j.1365-2699.2009.02078.x |bibcode=2009JBiog..36.1502M |s2cid=29713095 }}</ref> In additional, significant numbers of other [[lagomorph]]s may be taken, extending to occasional [[Granada hare]]s (''Lepus granatensis'') as well as accounts of Bonelli's eagles hunting [[European hare]]s (''Lepus europaeus'') in the Greek isles and [[Indian hare]]s (''Lepus nigricollis'') in the lower [[Himalayas]].<ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Handrinos>Handrinos, G., & Akriotis, T. (1997). ''The birds of Greece''. Christopher Helm.</ref><ref name= Miranzo>Miranzo, B.M. (2017). ''Ecología espacial del águila de Bonelli ("Aquila fasciata") en Aragón''. Universidad Complutense de Madrid, Departamento de Zoología y Antropología (Doctoral thesis).</ref>

===Gamebirds and pigeons===
[[File:Bonellis Eagle kill DSCN8964.jpg|thumb|Bonelli's eagle with a freshly caught [[grey junglefowl]]. Gamebirds such as junglefowl are favoured in the diet whenever available.]]
The main secondary wild prey species associated with Bonelli's eagles is the [[red-legged partridge]] (''Alectoris rufa''). Although at times capable of evading the attentions of eagles, this partridge occurs in conveys in the same mixed scrub that hold rabbits and is taken whenever the eagles are lucky enough to have the element of surprise. About 383 red-legged partridges were estimated to be hunted annually in one study area of southwestern Spain.<ref name= Martinez/><ref name= Moleon/><ref name= Moleon3/> In the large Spanish study of [[Catalonia]], French study of [[Provence]] and in southwest [[Portugal]], the red-legged partridge made up 9.57%, 11.6% and 17.2% of the diet by number, respectively.<ref name= Mayor/><ref name= Morvan/><ref name= Palma/> More so than any other prey type outside of western Europe, [[gamebird]]s such as partridges seem to be globally the most favoured prey type where available for Bonelli's eagle. In [[Cyprus]], a review of 528 prey items, revealed that the [[Chukar partridge|chukar]] (''Alectoris chukar'') was the main prey at 31.4% of the diet.<ref name= Iezekiel/> More than a dozen gamebirds have been detected in the foods of this species from [[Asia]] with at least a half dozen genera turning up in a few reviews of their ecology in India.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Naoroji/><ref name="Pande">{{Cite journal |last1=Pande |first1=Satish |last2=Yosef |first2=Reuven |last3=Morelli |first3=Federico |last4=Pawar |first4=Rajkumar |last5=Mone |first5=Ram |date=December 2018 |title=Diet and habitat affinities in six raptor species in India |journal=Avian Research |volume=9 |issue=1 |article-number=36 |doi=10.1186/s40657-018-0129-2 |doi-access=free}}</ref> At times, even adult [[Indian peafowl]] (''Pavo cristatus''), potentially weighing up to {{cvt|6|kg}}, have been dispatched by this species.<ref name= Naoroji/> In the [[Lesser Sunda Islands]], most eye-witness accounts of their hunting habits indicate that wild (or, on some islands, introduced) [[green junglefowl]] (''Gallus varius'') as well as [[Chicken|village chicken]]s (''Gallus gallus'') are likely to be the most important prey.<ref name= Trainor/> 

Beyond gamebirds, [[pigeon]]s are the other most significant avian prey type. The two larger European pigeons, the oft feral or domestic [[rock dove]] (''Columba livia'') and the [[common wood pigeon]] (''Columba palumbus''), are almost solely favoured among this group where encountered. In southwest Portugal, pigeons have surpassed rabbits (due to their disease-based decline) to become the most important prey. Here, attempts were made to parse the proportion of [[feral pigeon]]s that were taken against the number of [[domestic pigeon]]s (since [[pigeon fanciers]] frequently persecute this eagle due its allegedly heavily predation of domestic birds). Of the 1497 prey items overall, feral pigeons were found to comprise 30.1% of the food by number and 26% of the biomass while the domestic types made up only 9.7% of the diet by number and 7.2% of the biomass.<ref name= Palma/> In [[Catalonia]], [[Spain]], unidentified pigeons made up 17.8% of the foods and 17.4% of the biomass while identified common wood pigeons made up a further 6.24% of the number and 6.54% of the biomass, while a smaller study from the same area boosted wood pigeons to make up 11.3% of 524 prey items.<ref name= Mayor/><ref name="Resano">{{Cite journal |last1=Resano |first1=Jaime |last2=Hernández-Matías |first2=Antonio |last3=Real |first3=Joan |last4=Parés |first4=Francesc |date=December 2011 |title=Using Stable Isotopes To Determine Dietary Patterns In Bonelli's Eagle (Aquila fasciata) Nestlings |url=http://www.bioone.org/doi/abs/10.3356/JRR-11-13.1 |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |volume=45 |issue=4 |pages=342–352 |doi=10.3356/JRR-11-13.1 |bibcode=2011JRapR..45..342R }}</ref> In [[Cyprus]], rock and common wood pigeons collectively made up 27.7% of the diet.<ref name= Iezekiel/>

===Other birds===
[[File:Bonelli's Eagle (Aquila fasciata) (38711946095).jpg|thumb|left|A Bonelli's eagle being [[Mobbing (animal behavior)|mobbed]] by [[house crow]]s in [[Pakistan]], as the eagle is a common predator of corvids.]]
Other medium-sized birds are taken widely by Bonelli's eagles. A popular dietary choice in [[western Europe]] was [[yellow-legged gull]]s (''Larus michahellis''), weighing an estimated {{cvt|1119|g}}. In the 2,724 prey items in [[Provence]], this gull was second only to the rabbit in number, comprising 14.6% of the diet.<ref name=Mayor/><ref name=Morvan/> Other [[gull]]s are taken by Bonelli's eagles as well as wide diversity of other [[water bird]]s, including [[Rail (bird)|rails]], [[Eurasian stone-curlew]], [[northern lapwing]], [[sandpiper]]s, [[Procellariiformes|tubenose]]s, [[cormorant]]s and [[heron]]s.<ref name=Moleon/><ref name=Mayor/><ref name=Morvan/><ref name=Palma/><ref name=Resano2>Resano, J., Bayle, P., Real, J., Hernández, A., Vincent-Martin, N. & Ravayrol, A. (2012). ''Analyse du régime alimentaire de l'Aigle de Bonelli ''Hieraaetus fasciatus'' (Vieillot, 1822) pendant la saison de reproduction 2010 en France''. Université de Barcelone – Equip de Biologia de la Conservació, 1: 95–101.</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Daunt |first1=F. |last2=Wanless |first2=S. |last3=Harris |first3=M. P. |last4=Money |first4=L. |last5=Monaghan |first5=P. |date=June 2007 |title=Older and wiser: improvements in breeding success are linked to better foraging performance in European shags |journal=Functional Ecology |volume=21 |issue=3 |pages=561–567 |doi=10.1111/j.1365-2435.2007.01260.x |bibcode=2007FuEco..21..561D}}</ref> Water birds taken Bonelli's eagles may vary in size from wading birds as small as {{cvt|48|g}} [[common sandpiper]] (''Acitis hypoleucos'') and diving birds as small as {{cvt|174|g}} [[little grebe]]s (''Tachybaptus ruficollis'') to those as large as adults of {{cvt|3.18|kg}} [[painted stork]]s (''Ciconia leucocephala''), {{cvt|3.31|kg}} [[greylag goose]] (''Anser anser'') (though reportedly taken while injured by buckshot in India), and {{cvt|5.5|kg}} [[common crane]] (''Grus grus'').<ref name=Naoroji/><ref name=Palma/><ref name=Beton>{{Cite journal |last1=Beton |first1=D. |last2=Snape |first2=R. |last3=Saydam |first3=B. |date=2013 |title=Status and ecology of the Bonelli's Eagle, ''Aquila fasciatus'', in the Pentadaktylos Mountain Range, Cyprus (Aves: Falconiformes) |journal=Zoology in the Middle East |volume=59 |issue=2 |pages=123–130 |doi=10.1080/09397140.2013.810872 |s2cid=56299034}}</ref><ref name= Aviles>{{cite journal |last1=Avilés |first1=J.M. |last2=Sánchez |first2=J.M. |last3=Medina |first3=F.J. |year=1998 |title=Response of the crane ''Grus grus'' to potential predators in traditional wintering areas |journal=Vogelwarte |volume=39 |pages=202–203}}</ref> [[Corvid]]s, of a dozen or more species and up to the size of the {{cvt|1.1|kg}} [[common raven]] (''Corvus corax''), are taken in considerable numbers in differing parts of the range.<ref name=Moleon>{{Cite journal |last1=Moleón |first1=M. |last2=Sánchez-Zapata |first2=J. A. |last3=Gil-Sánchez |first3=J. M. |last4=Ballesteros-Duperón |first4=E. |last5=Barea-Azcón |first5=J. M. |last6=Virgós |first6=E. |date=2012 |title=Predator–prey relationships in a Mediterranean vertebrate system: Bonelli's eagles, rabbits and partridges |journal=Oecologia |volume=168 |issue=3 |pages=679–689 |doi=10.1007/s00442-011-2134-6 |pmid=21947548 |bibcode=2012Oecol.168..679M |s2cid=253983716}}</ref>

In Provence, [[Eurasian magpie]] (''Pica pica'') and [[western jackdaw]] (''Corvus monedula'') made up 10.17% and 9.95% of the diet respectively.<ref name=Morvan/> In Portugal, [[Eurasian jay]] comprised 7.5% by number but only 2.7% of the biomass.<ref name=Palma/> Corvids were the main prey for Bonelli's eagles in [[Georgia (country)|Georgia]], with the Eurasian magpie comprising 12.3% of the diet, though largely young were reportedly taken, and [[carrion crow]]s (''Corvus corone'') making up a further 10.76%.<ref name=Abuladze>{{cite book |author=Abuladze, A. |year=2013 |title=Birds of prey of Georgia. Materials towards a Fauna of Georgia Issue VI |place=Tbilisi |publisher=Printing House Lasha Khvichia |doi=10.13140/2.1.1904.3208 |doi-access=free}}</ref> In the [[Aegean islands]], carrion crows comprised 14.1% of the prey by number and 8.8% of the biomass, while south of Turkey in [[Cyprus]], western jackdaw comprised 7.6% of the foods.<ref name=Iezekiel/><ref name=Alivizatos/> Other avian prey groups taken in usually smaller numbers include [[cuckoo]]s, [[Swift (bird)|swift]]s, [[bustard]]s, [[European nightjar]], [[European bee-eater]], [[European roller]], [[Eurasian hoopoe]], [[woodpecker]]s, [[parrot]]s,<ref name=Palma/><ref name=Abuladze/><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Molina-Morales |first1=M. |last2=Martínez |first2=J. G. |last3=Avilés |first3=J. M. |date=2012 |title=Factors affecting natal and breeding magpie dispersal in a population parasitized by the great spotted cuckoo |journal=Animal Behaviour |volume=83 |issue=3 |pages=671–680 |doi=10.1016/j.anbehav.2011.12.011 |bibcode=2012AnBeh..83..671M |s2cid=53168437}}</ref> and various [[lark]]s, [[swallow]]s, [[Iberian grey shrike]], [[Dunnock|accentors]], at least 10 [[Old World flycatcher]] species, [[Thrush (bird)|thrush]]es, [[Meadow pipit|pipits]], [[starling]]s, [[Emberiza|buntings]], [[finch]]es and [[house sparrow]]s.<ref name=Morvan/><ref name=Palma/><ref name=Kumawat>{{cite journal |author1=Kumawat, R. |author2=Saran, R. P. |author3=Purohit, A. |year=2018 |title=Bonelli's Eagle: Records of predation on ''Varanus griseus'' and ''Ptyonoprogne concolor'' by ''Aquila fasciata'' in Agolai, Jodhpur, India |journal=Zoo's Print |volume=33 |issue=5 |pages=}}</ref> In total, about 130 bird species may be taken and birds as a whole almost always form the most ample part of the diet compared to other classes: 69.5% and 80.97% of the biomass in the [[south of France]], 67.7% in Georgia and 62.6% in [[Catalonia]].<ref name=Mayor/><ref name=Morvan/><ref name=Resano/>

===Other assorted prey===
Beyond the high significance of rabbits (and sometimes other lagomorphs), other mammals are rarely as important or diverse in the diet of Bonelli's eagles as birds are. A couple of rodents can be locally significant secondary prey, however. The [[red squirrel]] (''Sciurus vulgaris''), with a mean estimated mass in Spain of {{cvt|241|g}}, was reported in almost all [[western Europe]] studies, with about 130 reported as taken in studies from [[Provence]], [[France]].<ref name= Mayor/><ref name= Morvan/> The [[black rat]] (''Rattus rattus''), of similar size to the squirrel at an average of about {{cvt|200|g}} was an important secondary food source in islands south and east of [[Greece]], being the second most common prey species in [[Cyprus]] (15.5% of 528 prey items) and fifth most important prey species in the [[Aegean islands]].<ref name= Iezekiel/><ref name= Alivizatos/> In [[northwestern Africa]] such as [[Morocco]], it was reported that the [[fat sand rat]] (''Psammomys obesus''), another rodent of similar size, was amongst the favourite foods locally for Bonelli's eagles.<ref>Amezian, M., Irizi, A., Errati, A., Loran, H., El Khamlichi, R., Morandini, V., González, D. G., Garrido, J. R. (2015). ''Spanish Imperial Eagles and other eagles found electrocuted in Morocco and proposition of correction measures''.</ref> Other rodent species known in the diet of Bonelli's eagles have included other [[squirrel]]s, [[Common gundi|gundi]]s, assorted [[mice]], [[vole]]s, [[dormice]] and [[blind mole rat]]s.<ref name= Moleon/><ref name= Morvan/><ref name= Burger>Burger, J., Hiessler, N., Ponchon, C., & Vincent-Martin, N. (2013). ''Plan national d'actions en faveur de l'Aigle de Bonelli, Aquila fasciata (2014–2023)''. In Ministère de l'environnement et du développement durable et de l'energie.</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Jourdain |first1=F. C. R. |last2=Wallis |first2=H. M. |last3=Ratcliff |first3=F. R. |date=1915 |title=Notes on the Bird-Life of Eastern Algeria |journal=Ibis |volume=57 |issue=1 |pages=133–169 |doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1915.tb08184.x |url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/part/378267 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Németh |first1=Attila |last2=Hegyeli |first2=Zsolt |last3=Sendula |first3=T. |last4=Horváth |first4=M. |last5=Czabán |first5=D. |last6=Csorba |first6=G. |date=2016 |title=Danger underground and in the open – predation on blind mole rats ( R odentia: S palacinae) revisited |journal=Mammal Review |volume=46 |issue=3 |pages=204–214 |doi=10.1111/mam.12062 |bibcode=2016MamRv..46..204N}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Mate |first1=Isabel |last2=Barrull |first2=Joan |last3=Gosálbez |first3=Joaquim |last4=Ruiz-Olmo |first4=Jordi |last5=Salicrú |first5=Miquel |date=2015 |title=The role of the southern water vole ''Arvicola sapidus'' in the diet of predators: a review |journal=Mammal Review |volume=45 |issue=1 |pages=30–40 |doi=10.1111/mam.12030 |bibcode=2015MamRv..45...30M}}</ref> Beyond a few species of [[hedgehog]]s, additional mammalian prey for this species, although seldom taken, can be relatively large. They have been known to attack the young of various ungulates include [[blackbuck]] (''Antilope cervicapra''), [[chinkara]] (''Gazella bennettii''), [[domestic goat]]s (''Capra aegagrus hircus'') and [[domestic sheep]] (''Ovis aries'').<ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Moleon/><ref>Kumar, S. (1993). ''Bonelli's Eagle (Hieraatus fasciatus) killing a Blackbuck (Antilope cervicapra) fawn''. Journal of Raptor Research, 27(4), 218–219.</ref> In the [[Aegean islands]], live-caught but often young and small goat kids comprised 8.5% of the foods and 24.3% of the biomass at nests.<ref name= Alivizatos/> 

Among [[carnivora]]ns, Bonelli's eagles have reportedly attacked [[red fox]] (''Vulpes vulpes'') and [[wildcat]]s (''Felis silvestris'') (probably mostly kits and kittens of these two species) in western Europe as well as [[stone marten]]s (''Martes foina'') and assorted [[weasel]]s. Meanwhile, adult [[Bengal fox]] (''Vulpes bengalensis'') have reportedly been caught in India.<ref name= Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Moleon/><ref name= Naoroji/><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Palazón |first1=Santiago |last2=Camps |first2=David |last3=Carbonell |first3=Francesc |last4=Grajera |first4=Joan |date=2016 |title=Predation of the Weasel (Mustela nivalis) by diurnal raptors |url=https://www.secem.es/galemys/galemys-28-2016-n5/ |journal=Galemys, Spanish Journal of Mammalogy |volume=28 |pages=66–67 |doi=10.7325/Galemys.2016.N5}}</ref> In France and Spain, mammals overall comprised 34.8% and 26.1% of the diet, respectively, whereas in Georgia they made up 15.4% of the  diet.<ref name= Morvan/><ref name= Resano/><ref name= Resano2/><ref name= Abuladze/> [[Reptile]]s are usually secondary prey throughout the range. Though they are known to hunt [[snake]]s, Bonelli's eagles rarely hunt them and generally seem to pursue [[lizard]]s by preference. In Cyprus, [[starred agama]]s (''Laudakia stellio'') comprised 5.9% of the food, unidentified ''[[Lacerta (genus)|Lacerta]]'' lizards 10.76% of diet in [[Georgia (country)|Georgia]] (and reptiles altogether adding up to 16.9% of the food by number). Relatively large adult specimens of [[ocellated lizard]] (''Timon lepidus''), at {{cvt|228|g}} in mean body mass, made up 3.97% of the biomass and 7.05% by number in [[Catalonia]], [[Spain]].<ref name= Mayor/><ref name= Iezekiel/><ref name= Abuladze/> [[Desert monitor]] (''Varanus griseus'') and probably assorted other monitor lizards were reportedly amongst the leading prey for Bonelli's eagles in several parts of India.<ref name= Naoroji/><ref name= Pande/><ref name= Kumawat/> Minor prey includes [[Common toad|toads]] and possibly a few other types of [[amphibian]]. Potentially [[insect]]s and/or other [[invertebrate]]s may be taken but these may incidentally consumed (i.e. undigested food from the stomachs of prey).<ref name= Morvan/><ref name= Burger/>

===Interspecies predatory relationships===
[[File:Bonellis Eagle (Adult & Juv)-3732.jpg|thumb|While other birds of prey present some competition for Bonelli's eagles, intraspecific conflicts are the most significant. Here an adult escorts out a wandering juvenile from its range.]]
Bonelli's eagles frequently occur in range with multiple other eagles and competing predators. Almost certainly the most direct competitor from their European range to the [[Middle East]] is their much larger cousin, the [[golden eagle]]. Habitat preferences overlap between these two eagles with both species favouring rocky habitats, though the golden eagle regularly dwells at slightly higher elevations with [[alpine meadow]]s (though is as adaptive to low elevations as Bonelli's so long as habitat is favourable and undisturbed).<ref name=Watson>{{cite book |author=Watson, J. |title=The Golden Eagle |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=aj5MNvCkun0C |year=2010 |publisher=A&C Black |isbn=978-1-4081-1420-9}}</ref><ref name= Jordano/> Competition between the eagles has been reported in particular from Spain, in areas such as [[Sierra Morena]]. Both species excluded each other from mutual territories and had broadly overlapping food habits. However, Bonelli's eagle took more birds and golden eagle more singularly hunted rabbits. Mean distance between nests on a plot of {{cvt|2200|km2}} was found to be {{cvt|10.2|km}} for 8 pairs of golden eagles and {{cvt|11.4|km}} for 10 pairs of Bonelli's. The two can co-exist with sufficiently large ranges as long as they are able to maintain their own range, with the existence of trophic segregation (by size and the more avian based diet of Bonelli's) and the lag in the breeding periods, as these natural mechanisms would allow the coexistence of both species in the mountain.<ref name= Jordano/><ref name="Moreno-Rueda">{{Cite journal |last1=Moreno-Rueda |first1=G. |last2=Pizarro |first2=M. |last3=Ontiveros |first3=D. |last4=Pleguezuelos |first4=J. M. |date=2009 |title=The Coexistence of the Eagles ''Aquila chrysaetos'' and ''Hieraaetus fasciatus'' Increases with Low Human Population Density, Intermediate Temperature, and High Prey Diversity |journal=Annales Zoologici Fennici |volume=46 |issue=4 |pages=283–290 |doi=10.5735/086.046.0405 |s2cid=16513057}}</ref> 

Cases of golden eagles taking over prior Bonelli's eagles territories have been reported but usually golden eagles only takes up the prior Bonelli's territory when the latter vanishes due to unrelated (often [[Human impact on the environment|anthropogenic]]) causes not direct competition or usurpation. A minor negative effect has been probably correlated with golden eagles not infrequently attacking and displacing juvenile and subadult Bonelli's eagles and can tend to be behaviourally dominant in keeping with its larger size. This in turn presumably hampers the ability of Bonelli's to expand their range after declines and stabilize their population.<ref>Fernandez, C., & Insausti, J. A. (1990). ''Golden eagles take up territories abandoned by Bonelli's eagles in Northern Spain''. Journal of Raptor Research, 24, 124–125.</ref><ref>Dobado-Berrios,  P.M., Álvarez, R. & Leiva, A. (1998). ''El Águila Perdicera en la provincia de Córdoba''. Quercus, 154: 48–49.</ref><ref>Ontiveros, D. (2000). ''Ecología de una población de Águila Perdicera (Hieraaetus fasciatus) del sureste ibérico: plan de conservación''. Tesis doctoral. Universidad de Granada. Granada.</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Carrete |first1=Martina |last2=Sánchez-Zapata |first2=José A. |last3=Calvo |first3=J- F. |last4=Lande |first4=R. |date=2005 |title=Demography and habitat availability in territorial occupancy of two competing species |journal=Oikos |volume=108 |issue=1 |pages=125–136 |doi=10.1111/j.0030-1299.2005.12942.x |bibcode=2005Oikos.108..125C |hdl=10261/64929 |hdl-access=free}}</ref><ref>Cheylan, G. (1973). ''[Notes on the competition between the Golden Eagle Aquila chrysaetos and Bonelli's Eagle Hieraaetus fasciatus]''. Alauda, 41: 203–212.</ref> Further east, in [[Israel]], Bonelli's and golden eagles are competitors as well. In the dry, barren [[Negev desert]], golden eagles nests were found {{cvt|13.1|km}} apart and Bonelli's were scarce. In the [[Judean desert]], which has more annual rainfall and more available prey, the distance between golden eagle nests averaged {{cvt|16|km}} and Bonelli's eagle easily outnumbered them. Apparently, Bonelli's eagle exceptionally outcompeted its larger cousin here due to a subtle topographic variation in the habitat.<ref name= Bahat>Bahat, O. 1989. European rabbits have a huge range of predators in the [[Iberian Peninsula]], with at least 30 different species known to hunt the once densely populated lagomorph.<ref name=Moleon/><ref name=Moleon3/><ref name= Palomares/> Besides the overlapping ranges of Bonelli's and golden eagles, most other birds of prey that hunt rabbits extensively are partitioned from the potential depletive effect of competition by differences in habitat preferences, hunting techniques and temporal activity. Beyond the specialized mammalian predator, the [[Iberian lynx]] (''Lynx pardinus''), some of the other most specialized predators of wild rabbits are Bonelli's eagles, golden eagles, [[Spanish imperial eagle]]s (''Aquila adalberti'') and [[Eurasian eagle-owl]]s.<ref name=Moleon/><ref name=Moleon3/><ref name=Palomares/><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Delibes-Mateos |first1=Miguel |last2=Redpath |first2=S. ''Owls of the Northern Hemisphere''. The MIT Press, 0262220350.</ref> Elsewhere, higher import has been applied for rabbits in the local diet of golden eagles as well as for Spanish imperial eagles.<ref name= Jordano/><ref>Sánchez, R., Margalida, A., González, L. Along with northern goshawks, golden eagles and Eurasian eagle-owls, Bonelli's eagle is considered a "super predator" in the European region due to its habit of hunting other predators. In contrast to the other birds of prey, they are somewhat less commonly at high predator status compared to goshawks (most common predator of other diurnal raptors in studies), golden eagles (most common predator of [[mesopredator]] mammals), and eagle-owl (most common predator of other owls).<ref name=Lourenco>{{Cite journal |last1=Lourenço |first1=R. |last2=Santos |first2=S.
